title: Lack of `cpuset` availability should be a warning rather than a fatal error?
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

I've recently starting trying to use `podman machine` VMs to build on Apple Silicon systems.

Ideally, the same invocation would work here as anywhere, with `podman` coping with any lack of feature-parity between `podman machine` and bare-metal `podman` scenarios.

With `podman machine`, whether launched on the host or within the `podman machine` VM (to work around the current lack of volume-mount support from the host), if I launch a container with:

```
podman container run --cpuset-cpus 1-7 ...
```

... then the result is the fatal error:

```
Error: OCI runtime error: the requested cgroup controller `cpuset` is not available
```

I'd suggest that if it's a known issue that CPU sets aren't currently supported in this situation, then the above error should be reduced to a warning, and the container should still be started (albeit without the CPU set restriction applied).

If this is felt to be too dangerous to apply in general, then perhaps a new `--permit-missing-features[=all|feature, ...]` argument (or similar) could be used to state the intention of the developer that running the container is the significant action, and that other constraints are nice-to-haves?

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      3.4.0
API Version:  3.4.0
Go Version:   go1.16.8
Built:        Thu Sep 30 19:40:10 2021
OS/Arch:      linux/arm64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md)**


Yes

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

`podman machine` on macOS & `podman machine` VM on Mac Mini M1.

comments.body: if `--cpuset-...` is not available on machine I think erroring out is the proper response. Maybe a more up front message informing you the flag isn't valid and a possible update to the man pages for machine compatible commands.. all in all though a warning would not suffice here as the final product of that would not be what the user specified.
@baude do you agree on the front that this should be a fatal error? I feel like if the container cannot be started as specified, starting a container w/ different params shouldn't be allowed.
This should definitely be an error.  Now if the machine is not setup correctly for certain settings, then that is a different matter.

Well, It would make sense to me if machine doesn't support cpuset information as well as some other linux based resource limits, but if they are technically supported then yes this is a deeper issue. I think if they aren't supported, adding some denotation of machine supported and not supported flags/commands would be helpful.

the error is coming from the OCI runtime.  It will be difficult to know from Podman what features are really supported.

I think we should not change the current behavior.  If the user specifies some limits that cannot be honored then Podman/OCI runtime must fail.  `permit-missing-features` seems difficult to implement
I agree if a user asks for a cgroup feature and it does not work we should fail.
After remarks I think we can close this as a `WONTFIX`. @srcshelton Please feel free to reopen if you think otherwise.

My only concern is, is there something that we need to change configuration within the podman-machine (Fedora CoreOS) to turn on all of the cgroups that the user could use?  IE If this is a configuration issue in the machine, we could fix it in the ignition?
